corrected_note:
Resident: Keva Okafor, Room 7. Morning shift handover assessment commenced at approximately 07:15 on Thursday.

Patient experienced an extremely disturbed night – recurring incident (multiple episodes). Patient was up from bed at least four separate times, as documented by night staff Assumpta. On each occasion Patient attempted to walk to the toilet unassisted, contrary to the falls‑prevention care plan which requires use of the call bell and waiting for assistance before standing. Bed‑sensor alarm activated on three of the four occasions. At approximately 01:45, Assumpta recorded that the sensor mat was found folded at the foot of the bed, indicating Patient deliberately repositioned it. This behaviour mirrors incidents on the preceding Monday and Wednesday and constitutes a recurring pattern of deliberate circumvention of safety equipment. Documented as a recurring safety incident and flagged to the unit manager and physiotherapist for urgent review of the falls‑prevention plan.

At approximately 03:20, Patient was found standing in the centre of her room without her Zimmer frame and without non‑slip footwear. Assumpta guided Patient safely back to bed and completed a post‑standing assessment; no injury identified. Patient was very agitated and repeatedly stated she needed to find her sister Nora (deceased 2009). Patient frequently calls for her sister during periods of nocturnal confusion, as noted in the dementia care plan. Sundowning behaviour has been escalating over the past two weeks; this pattern has been recorded and escalated to Consultant Geriatrician Dr Flanagan.

Dr Flanagan reviewed Patient on Tuesday and documented moderate‑to‑severe Alzheimer’s disease with nocturnal behavioural disturbances consistent with disease progression.

Patient refused warfarin 3 mg, bisoprolol 2.5 mg, and lansoprazole 30 mg this morning — fourth consecutive morning of refusal (recurring). Patient pushed medication cup away, turned face to wall, and stated she is not sick and does not need tablets. A second refusal attempt was made at 07:45 by offering tablets hidden in a teaspoon of strawberry jam; patient spat out jam and tablets. Patient repeatedly refusing oral medication — ongoing non‑compliance (fourth consecutive morning). Dr Kelly reviewed INR 2.8 (therapeutic range 2–3) and was satisfied with anticoagulation control. No new medication prescribed; review planned in 2 weeks. Capacity assessment completed; repeated refusals may represent expressed wishes rather than impaired capacity. BP 190/97 mmHg at 07:00 and 186/93 mmHg at 07:15 left arm — both significantly above baseline ~145/85 mmHg. Dr Kelly bleeped to discuss options, including liquid warfarin formulation or reassessment of anticoagulation given falls risk.

BP elevated — likely related to missed bisoprolol dose; patient refused evening medications yesterday and did not take bisoprolol last night at 18:00 and this morning. Patient repeatedly refusing oral medication — ongoing non‑compliance (second refusal). Call placed to Dr Kelly; BP readings documented for urgent review today.  

RR 17 breaths/min, O2 sats 96% on room air — within normal limits. HR 88 bpm, irregular, baseline atrial fibrillation. Temp 36°C.6 °C. Blood glucose not monitored (non‑diabetic).  

Full body skin assessment performed during personal care. New non‑blanchable erythema 2.5 cm × 2 cm on left heel, grade 1 pressure injury — five‑layer foam heel protector dressing applied, documented in wound care chart. Left heel elevated on foam wedge pillow; pressure‑relief care plan updated to include heel off‑loading at each repositioning.  

Sacral dressing (Duoderm) placed Tuesday found partially peeled at edges; sacral area cleaned and fresh sacral foam dressing applied. Underlying skin shows grade 1 redness, no progression to grade 2.  

Resident on nutritional alert (red tray). Requires total nursing assistance for meals due to Parkinson’s disease affecting fine motor control. Breakfast: approx 75 % of porridge with honey, 2 cups tea, 150 mL orange juice.

Swallowing function last assessed by Speech and Language Therapy (SLT) in February; resident deemed safe for normal textures with Level 2 thickened fluids. This morning resident coughed on three consecutive sips of thickened tea and once on a spoonful of porridge – a change from recent pattern. Suspected swallowing deterioration documented; urgent SALT referral placed for review within 48 h.

Weight 51.2 kg this morning, down from 52.1 kg on Monday (loss 0.9 kg in 3 days). Unintentional weight loss >5 % in one month reported to dietitian; documented as nutritional concern.

Urine output charted by night nurse: approx. 540 ml over 8‑hour night shift (low). Urine this morning concentrated, dark amber, indicating dehydration. Oral fluid target increased to 1500 ml for day shift; fluid balance chart updated.

Resident’s urine noted with offensive odor and increased confusion; possible UTI queried. Midstream urine sample sent for culture and sensitivity. No temperature spike observed; acknowledge blunted febrile response in dementia.

corrected_note:
Aishlin Varghese, R12. Late‑morning assessment completed at 11:50 am on Tuesday.  

At 09:10, HC assistant Deirdre observed patient actively pulling at her PEG tube with her right hand; tube appeared displaced. Patient self‑manipulated PEG tube — recurring incident (fifth episode in 14 days). External bumper displaced ≈2.5 cm outward from skin level; small amount of clear gastric fluid leaking around stoma. Nurse stabilised tube manually.  

Dr Murphy attended at 09:25, confirmed partial dislodgement and noted balloon/bumper at risk of migration if further tension applied. Tube stable; no immediate surgical intervention required. Interventional radiology review recommended within 24 h to assess tube position and stoma integrity.  

Padded safety mitten applied to dominant right hand following formal restraint assessment; verbal consent obtained from daughter Maura at 09:40 (written consent pending). Documented as restrictive practice per unit policy; to be reviewed at MDT meeting Friday morning. Clinical team notified of each occurrence.

Palliative Care Clinical Nurse Specialist Catherine reviewed Aishlin on Friday last week in the context of recent incidents and raised the question of whether continuing enteral feeding via PEG tube remains in Aishlin’s best interest given her increasing resistance and distress around the tube. This conversation was held with Maura, Aishlin’s next of kin and healthcare proxy. Maura has asked for more time to consider the options, and a formal best‑interest meeting has been scheduled for next Monday at 14:00 with Dr Murphy (Palliative Care Consultant), the speech‑and‑language therapist, and the dietitian attending. All attendees have been confirmed available and the meeting is entered in the diary.

Aishlin had her PEG feed running at 75 mL/hr of Fresubin Original since midnight last night. Following this morning’s PEG displacement event, Dr Murphy directed that the feed be held until interventional radiology review is completed. She is receiving 30 mL water flushes q6h through the tube to maintain patency, but no further feed is running.

Last blood glucose (BG) check at 11:30 was 9.2 mmol/L. She is on NovoRapid 6 units TID with feeds and Lantus 12 units nightly. Because feeds are held, mealtime NovoRapid doses are not being administered today; Dr Murphy verbally authorised holding NovoRapid for today with hourly BG monitoring to detect hypoglycaemia. BG at 11:50 was 8.8 mmol/L (acceptable). At 10:00 BG was 14.2 mmol/L (elevated); stress hyperglycaemia was considered given the distressing morning incident. The pattern of elevated morning BG over the past week is documented, and Dr Murphy has requested a diabetes specialist nurse review this week.

Aishlin’s bowels have not opened in five days; her usual pattern is every two to three days.

Resident on lactulose 15 mL BID and Movicol 1 sachet daily — regimen currently insufficient. Two glycerine suppositories administered at 10:30 with patient consent; tolerated without distress. At 11:45 patient passed a small amount of hard stool, providing some relief; abdomen remains mildly distended and tympanic on percussion. Documented and dietitian referral made to review fibre and fluid intake. Water flushes increased to 80 mL QID; bowel response to be reassessed this afternoon. Communication remains significantly impaired secondary to cerebrovascular accident 17 months ago; expressive dysphasia predominant with relatively preserved comprehension. This morning patient became very distressed during PEG tube incident, repeatedly attempting to communicate (possible “maura”, “door”, or “more”) despite multiple attempts with communication board. Patient subsequently agitated, vocalising loudly and banging cot side with left hand. Staff provided 30 min of calm reassurance, proximity, and preferred Irish traditional music via radio; agitation gradually subsided to manageable level. Music intervention noted as most effective non‑pharmacological strategy per nursing documentation and prioritized in dementia behaviour care plan. BP 114/72 mmHg at midday, slightly below baseline 130/75 mmHg. Orthostatic assessment: sitting BP 108/68 mmHg after lying — mild orthostatic hypotension.

All care staff verbally briefed: Resident Aishlin must not be transferred to wheelchair without a slow, graduated position change supervised by a registered nurse. Tissue viability nurse Patricia reviewed the PEG stoma site on Thursday, recommended silver nitrate application for perilesional granulation tissue, and applied silver nitrate on Thursday and Monday. Granulation tissue has reduced slightly but persists. Patricia is aware of today’s displacement event and has been contacted by phone. Patricia will review the stoma in person tomorrow morning and will advise on further wound management. Wound assessment chart and photographs updated today.

Shivawn Pillai, Room 3A – afternoon assessment (02:15 Wed).

At 10:42 am, HCA Patricia found Shivawn on the bathroom floor of her ensuite. She had risen from the toilet unassisted, fallen backward, struck her lower back against the edge of the bath, and landed on the tiled floor.

Shivawn is on a 2‑person assist protocol for all transfers, including toilet transfers, instituted after a fall six weeks ago. The protocol requires a nurse or HCA to be present and assisting at all times and the bed sensor alarm to be activated when she is in bed. The bed sensor alarm was not triggered prior to this fall, suggesting it may not have been re‑activated after the 08:00 am personal care session. I have spoken with morning‑shift Nurse Sinead and HCA Brigid (who performed the morning care); both recall completing the routine but cannot confirm with certainty that the sensor was re‑activated. The incident has been escalated to the Unit Manager and a full incident review is underway.

Immediate clinical assessment was performed at 10:50 am by me in conjunction with the attending physician, Dr Farrell, who was present on the ward. No apparent fracture of the hip, knees, wrists, or spine. Patient is weight‑bearing on both legs with her usual Zimmer frame, but appears guarded and moves more slowly than her baseline. A small contusion approximately 4 cm × 3 cm over the left posterior iliac crest is tender to palpation, with no deformity or crepitus.

Ice pack applied for 20 minutes — bruise to be reassessed at 1600. Superficial abrasion over right knee ~1.5 cm cleaned with normal saline and dressed with non‑adherent dressing and light crepe bandage. Neurological observations scheduled: hourly for first 4 h post‑fall, then every 2 h for next 8 h per post‑fall protocol. GCS 15/15 at 14:15; pupils equal 4 mm, reactive to light (direct and consensual). Patient denies headache, nausea, visual disturbance. Note: moderate vascular dementia; poor historian — caveat recorded in post‑fall assessment form. Family notified at 11:15; son Eoin contacted, expressed concern and will visit tonight. Son inquired about X‑ray; explained decision rests with Dr Farrell, who will provide recommendation at tomorrow morning ward round. Dr Farrell documented interim note: urgent X‑ray not indicated at this stage; situation to be reviewed. Patient on rivaroxaban 20 mg daily for non‑valvular atrial fibrillation — elevated bleeding risk flagged in incident documentation and handover; Dr Farrell aware. Diabetes mellitus type 2 managed with metformin 500 mg BID and gliclazide 80 mg QD AM. Pre‑lunch capillary blood glucose at 12:30 was 17.4 mmol/L.

Resident Shivawn could not recall breakfast consumption — typical of her memory pattern. Kitchen staff confirmed full breakfast was provided but largely uneaten.  

Elevated blood glucose despite reduced intake — attributed to stress hyperglycaemia from recent fall and associated pain. Recheck scheduled at 1600 hrs; pre‑dinner glucose will guide any insulin dose adjustment.  

Resident emotionally distressed since fall, crying intermittently this afternoon and repeatedly requesting husband Seamus (deceased 2 years ago). Dementia care plan flags discussion of Seamus’s death as sensitive; nurse employed gentle validation, acknowledging Seamus’s love and encouraging rest — moderately effective. Pastoral care coordinator contacted to arrange chaplain visit.  

At 1400 hrs, pink non‑blanchable area ~3 cm diameter noted on coccyx, not present on prior skin check — grade 1 pressure injury, likely related to increased chair time due to fall precautions. Thin bordered hydrofibre dressing applied by nurse; repositioning chart updated. Resident to be repositioned q2h this afternoon and overnight.  

Urinary catheter (inserted 2 weeks ago for retention secondary to neurogenic bladder) draining dark amber concentrated urine; output since 0600 hrs 280 mL, low volume.

Encouraged oral fluid intake; resident Shivawn has consumed approximately 600 mL fluids to date. Will monitor fluid balance through the afternoon and evening and will contact Dr Farrell if output does not improve.

corrected_note:
Resident Feekra Krishnan, Room 9 – early shift handover (08:00).  

At approximately 03:15 h, acute respiratory decompensation documented by staff Niamh: patient sitting bolt upright, pale, diaphoretic, using neck and intercostal muscles, unable to speak full sentences, gripping cot side. Niamh commenced O2 4 L/min via nasal cannula, activated emergency call system and contacted on‑call physician Dr O’Connor by phone.  

Dr O’Connor arrived at ~03:27 h; O2 saturation 89% on 4 L nasal cannula, deemed inadequate. O2 increased to 8 L/min via 35% Venturi mask. Reassessment after 15 min showed saturation 94% (baseline 95‑97% on room air).  

Portable chest X‑ray performed at ~04:00 h; report: bilateral basal air‑space opacification (right > left), small bilateral pleural effusions, increased vascular markings consistent with cardiogenic pulmonary oedema in setting of known ischaemic cardiomyopathy and congestive cardiac failure.  

Dr O’Connor administered furosemide 40 mg IV via peripherally inserted central catheter (PICC) at 04:15 h. Blood drawn for urgent biochemistry: urea & electrolytes, full blood count, troponin, brain natriuretic peptide, and arterial blood gases.

ABG at 04:30: pH 7.33, PaCO₂ 51 mmHg, PaO₂ 81 mmHg on 8 L/min via Venturi mask — mild type 2 respiratory failure with compensated respiratory acidosis per Dr O'Connor.  

At 05:30, Niamh documented that Feekra's breathing had eased noticeably; she was less distressed, able to lie back slightly while remaining at ~45° head elevation. Saturations at 05:30 were 96% on 6 L/min via Venturi mask; Dr O'Connor satisfied with response to furosemide.  

At 08:00 assessment: saturations 95% on 6 L/min Venturi mask, RR 24 breaths/min (elevated vs usual 14‑16), mild dyspnoea on speech—able to complete ~2/3 of a sentence before pausing, improved from overnight. Pulse 104 bpm, irregularly irregular (chronic AF) faster than usual controlled rate 70‑80 bpm. BP 162/98 mmHg, elevated vs baseline 130/80 mmHg. Temp 37°C.1 °C. Cardiac auscultation: irregular rhythm with S3 gallop at apex, indicating cardiac decompensation, not present on prior formal cardiac assessment. Finding documented and flagged to Dr O'Connor; consultant cardiologist Dr Walsh requested for urgent inpatient review today.  

PICC line in right antecubital fossa, placed 1 week ago for IV furosemide; PICC site inspected this morning.

Exit site clean, non‑inflamed; no erythema tracking along arm; no tenderness on palpation above insertion site. Dressing intact, dry, last changed Tuesday. PICC line patent with good blood return confirmed by staff aspirating 2 mL dark venous blood before furosemide administered by staff. Second dose of furosemide 40 mg administered via PICC at 06:00 h per overnight escalation prescription by Dr O’Connor; additional 40 mg prescribed for 12:00 h today. Urine output from urethral catheter midnight‑08:00 h was 1,120 mL, indicating excellent diuretic response. Catheter bag changed by staff at 08:00 h; urine currently clear, pale yellow, suggesting improving hydration. Fluid balance over past 24 h shows approx ‑400 mL, appropriate for diuresis targeting pulmonary congestion. Plan: monitor for excessive fluid depletion; staff will check lying BP and assess for signs of hypovolaemia at midday. Routine morning medications reviewed. Bisoprolol 2.5 mg withheld this morning pending Dr O’Connor review due to elevated HR and concern for contribution to cardiac decompensation; documented as withheld with clinical reason. Spironolactone 25 mg administered by staff as prescribed. Ramipril 2.5 mg administered by staff. Apixaban 2.5 mg BID administered by staff as prescribed (stroke risk > bleeding risk). Patient is type 1 diabetic on basal‑bolus regimen; fasting glucose this morning 4.8 mmol/L.

Novorapid 4 units administered with breakfast. Oral intake very poor: half a glass of Fortisip and a few spoonfuls of scrambled egg. Monitoring closely for hypoglycaemia due to reduced carbohydrate intake relative to insulin dose. 10:00 am BM 6.2 mmol/L — acceptable. Feekra and family very distressed overnight. Nurse telephoned daughter Anitha at approximately 07:15 am; Anitha was upset and is arriving with brother Rajiv this morning. Advance care directive remains in force, specifying no hospital transfer and no resuscitation; reviewed with family in November with continued agreement. Directive placed in front of Feekra’s file and at bedside; all nursing and medical staff informed.

corrected_note:
Mev Nambiar, 82 y/o, advanced Alzheimer’s dementia (severe stage), COPD GOLD 3, type 2 diabetes mellitus managed with oral agents only (no insulin), hypertension, chronic constipation. Regular meds: Fostair 2 puffs BID via aerochamber, Spiriva Respimat 1 puff daily, Salbutamol 2 puffs PRN q4h, Metformin 500 mg BID with meals, Sitagliptin 100 mg daily, Amlodipine 5 mg daily, Macrogol powder 1 sachet BID.  

At approx 11:45 am, Patient attempted to climb over right‑side cot rail while HCA Deirdre was momentarily out of the room. Deirdre heard a noise, returned, found Patient with right leg over the rail and upper body angled dangerously over the edge. Deirdre called for assistance; colleague Attracta attended within seconds. Together they safely manoeuvred Patient back onto the mattress. No fall occurred and immediate assessment identified no injury. Patient Weight 74 kg; estimated drop from cot rail to floor ≈80 cm; osteoporosis risk present. This is the third attempt in eight days for Patient to go over or through cot side barriers — recurring safety event. Incident fully documented as a patient safety event and reported to unit manager and on‑call physician Dr Walsh, who reviewed Patient at 12:30.

Multidisciplinary meeting (unit manager, physiotherapist, occupational therapist, and author) agreed on immediate safety measures: crash mats placed on both sides of the bed and at the foot of the bed; direct observation protocol instituted—staff member to remain visible and within arm’s reach of Resident Mev during waking hours for the next 48 h pending formal assessment; formal restraint and least‑restrictive‑practice review scheduled for Friday morning with all relevant disciplines. Any additional physical restraint beyond the existing low‑set cot sides will require a best‑interest assessment with family involvement and MDT agreement per the unit’s restraint‑minimisation policy—process initiated by contacting Resident Mev’s daughter, Chitra (next of kin and healthcare decision‑maker); Chitra informed of this morning’s event and the proposed review and will attend Friday.

Respiratory status: Resident Mev’s condition deteriorated today. RR 26 breaths/min at 14:00; audible expiratory wheeze bilaterally; use of accessory muscles on exertion. SpO₂ on room air 91% (baseline 94‑95% on RA). O₂ therapy commenced at 2 L/min via nasal cannula; SpO₂ improved to 94% on supplemental O₂. Salbutamol inhaler used six times since 07:00 (three times usual daily frequency). Temp 37°C.9 °C at 14:00. New productive cough with green‑yellow mucopurulent sputum on each episode (previously dry cough).

Sputum sample obtained for culture and sensitivity; discussed with Dr Walsh, who concurs that a lower respiratory tract infection is likely and prescribed doxycycline 100 mg BID pending results. Doxycycline administered at 14:00 today. Respiratory observation chart initiated with q2h recording of respiratory rate, SpO2, and breath sounds.

Behavioral symptoms of dementia severe today — persistent restlessness, continuous vocalisation of “mammy mammy mammy,” and tearing at clothing and bedding. Patient self‑removed hospital gown on three occasions — recurring incident; patient was redressed each time and is now wearing a wrap‑around fastening gown, more difficult to remove unassisted. Music therapy (RTE lyric FM) partially effective today, likely attenuated by chest infection.

Discussed with Dr Walsh the option of a small PRN dose of lorazepam for acute severe agitation if non‑pharmacological measures fail; Dr Walsh will hold off pending assessment of antibiotic response and agitation improvement. Plan documented.

Oral intake critically reduced. Breakfast untouched. Lunch: patient accepted two spoonfuls yogurt and one sip thickened fluid, then repeatedly turned head away despite multiple gentle attempts by nursing staff. Two scheduled Fortisip Compact 160 mL drinks both refused. Dietitian notified.

Weight 47.3 kg recorded 3 days ago. Resident on dietitian‑supervised nutritional management for 6 weeks due to ongoing weight loss pattern.

Current acute illness likely to worsen nutritional status. Dietitian requested for urgent phone review today to advise on possible escalation of nutritional support within the dementia care plan and advance care directive, which specifies no NG or enteral feeding. Bowel movements opened this morning for the first time in three days; patient passed a small amount of hard stool, documented in bowel chart. Macrogol dose continued.

corrected_note:
Resident Roree Okafor, 68‑year‑old male, admitted 8 months ago following a severe left middle cerebral artery (MCA) ischemic stroke. Residual deficits include left‑sided hemiplegia, mixed expressive and receptive dysphasia, right‑sided neglect, and cognitive impairment. He is the only male resident on the unit; privacy and dignity considerations are noted for intimate care.

Significant behavioural incident occurred last night (approximately 23:50–02:00). At ~23:30 the resident became increasingly agitated in bed, banging cot sides and vocalising loudly. Staff (Cormac) assessed the resident and found no obvious physical cause; the resident was non‑cooperative. Agitation escalated, and at ~00:00 the resident removed all clothing, including continence pad, and stood naked at the doorway, shouting into the corridor. Staff employed repeated verbal de‑escalation and physical presence; the resident remained resistive at the doorway for ~15 minutes.

On‑call physician Dr Patel reviewed the resident at 00:20, assessing acute agitation with possible nocturnal delirium superimposed on chronic cognitive impairment. Dr Patel prescribed lorazepam 1 mg PO as a one‑off dose, administered by staff at 00:40. The resident gradually settled over the next 40 minutes and was back in bed by ~01:30. The resident slept soundly from 01:30 until the 08:00 review. The cause of the agitation remains unclear.

Possible contributing factors: pain from spastic left arm with increased contracture; urinary discomfort due to catheter kink at leg‑bag connection noted by Cormac at 23:00 and corrected; environmental noise in corridor from another resident requiring emergency medical attention at that time; underlying neurological and cognitive changes from prior stroke predisposing to nocturnal delirium. Documented and to be reviewed by MDT at Tuesday afternoon case conference. Lorazepam prescribed as a one‑off; no regular benzodiazepine in place. Dr Patel documented that if similar episodes recur, low‑dose regular anxiolytic should be discussed at MDT level and not prescribed in isolation. Resident now sleeping at 09:00 with regular unlaboured respirations, RR 14/min, good colour, pink lips. Plan: allow sleep until 10:00, then gently rouse for chest physiotherapy at 10:30 with physiotherapist Fionnuala, important for respiratory management and pneumonia prevention (most serious post‑stroke complication). Morning medications scheduled at 09:00; prescribing team to advise whether to give on waking or delay to usual time – call placed to Dr Patel. Resident’s medication regimen: clopidogrel 75 mg daily (secondary stroke prevention); atorvastatin 40 mg nightly (lipid management); ramipril 10 mg daily (hypertension, LV systolic dysfunction); spironolactone 50 mg daily (cardiac failure); dapagliflozin 10 mg daily (type 2 diabetes and cardiac protection); metformin 500 mg twice daily; linagliptin 5 mg daily. Complex regimen noted.

Insulin degludec 20 units SC administered at bedtime. Morning BM 8.3 mmol/L at 08:30, measured by Cormac — within target range 6–10 mmol/L. Blood pressure 145/88 mmHg at 08:30 — target set by stroke consultant Dr Lyons <130/80 mmHg, currently not achieved. Stroke rehabilitation progressing slowly. Physiotherapy goals: maintain ROM in left shoulder and hip; achieve transfers with single‑person assist (instead of two‑person hoist); attain left‑leg weight‑bearing during standing transfers. Occupational therapy goals: maximise independence in upper‑limb activities, including self‑feeding with adapted utensils on right side; cognitive rehabilitation focusing on attention and memory tasks. Speech‑language therapist last review 5 months ago: dysphagia assessed as safe for normal textures with mildly thickened fluids; communication assessed via Aphasia Impact Questionnaire. New referral submitted today for swallowing reassessment and communication‑strategy review. Long‑term silicone urethral catheter 16 Fr, inserted 6 weeks ago for neurogenic bladder secondary to stroke; last changed 3 weeks ago, next change due in 3 weeks. Urine clear, pale yellow; no signs of blockage or infection; no catheter‑associated discomfort documented. Leg bag clean, junction secure. Personal care today requires full assistance with washing, dressing, oral care, and catheter hygiene. Continence pad soiled overnight and changed by Cormac.

Full skin integrity assessment to be completed as part of morning care.

corrected_note:
Leesha Iyer, 84, resident, Room 6. Assessment at 16:30 h Friday. Emergency admission yesterday afternoon following witnessed fall in kitchen. Neighbour Mrs Hennessy found patient on kitchen floor in semiconscious state at approx 14:45 h, called 999. Patient transported by ambulance to Mater Hospital ED. History from neighbour and patient (when alert) indicates fall while stepping backward from kitchen counter, striking occipital region on table edge. Estimated LOC 15–30 min (approximate). CT head non‑contrast performed in ED: no acute intracranial haemorrhage, no skull fracture; chronic right subdural hygroma measuring ~4 mm noted – chronic finding, follow‑up imaging in 6 weeks unless clinical deterioration. Neurosurgical registrar consulted; advised conservative management with close neurological monitoring. Occipital laceration ~3.5 cm closed with four metallic staples by staff in ED. Patient lives alone, no family in Ireland; nearest relative nephew Vijay Iyer (London) contacted by phone, travel to Ireland scheduled for tomorrow morning, expected arrival midday. Next‑of‑kin details confirmed in admission documentation.

Wound assessed today: clean, dry, no surrounding erythema or discharge; all four staples intact and appropriately positioned. Wound review and staple removal scheduled for day 10 (next Monday). Resident Leesha is on warfarin for paroxysmal atrial fibrillation. Current regimen confirmed with GP Dr Patil: 5 mg on Monday, Wednesday, Friday, Sunday; 4 mg on Tuesday, Thursday, Saturday. INR on admission (yesterday) 3.9 ( supratherapeutic; target 2–3 ) in context of head injury with chronic subdural hygroma. Dr Costello ordered warfarin to be withheld for 3 days with repeat INR in 72 h. No vitamin K or prothrombin complex concentrate prescribed; conservative management chosen given normal CT, absence of neurological deterioration, and haemostatic pressure dressing applied. Plan to reassess anticoagulation once INR is therapeutic and clinical situation re‑evaluated. Instruction to transfer resident immediately by emergency ambulance if any neurological deterioration is detected is placed at the front of the nursing care plan and communicated verbally at each shift handover. Neurological observations performed every 2 h and recorded on standardized neuro‑obs chart. At 16:00, GCS 14/15 (orientation deficit: patient believes today is Wednesday rather than Friday and thinks she is in a hospital).

Resident fully oriented to person. Pupils 3 mm bilaterally, equal and reactive to light (direct and consensual). Grip strength symmetric bilaterally; plantar responses flexor bilaterally. No facial asymmetry. Reports residual headache 2–3/10 today (down from 5–6/10 yesterday morning). Paracetamol 1 g PO at 14:00, reported helpful. BP 144/80 mmHg at 16:00; HR 68 bpm, regular; RR 15/min; Temp 36°C.7 °C. Bilateral essential tremor, ~20‑year history, documented in GP notes; more prominent when anxious and noticeable today while holding cup of tea. Provided weighted cup and non‑slip mat on over‑bed table, facilitating independent drinking. Fully continent. Mobilising independently with walking stick; ambulated ~15 m in corridor this afternoon with staff supervision, no stumbling. Physiotherapist Marie scheduled for formal mobility and falls‑risk assessment tomorrow morning. Resident very anxious and distressed by unfamiliar environment; repeatedly expresses fear about outcomes and return home (recurring anxiety). Resident Conker O’Connell, 76 y/o, moderate‑stage Parkinson’s disease with Parkinson’s disease dementia diagnosed 14 months ago (Memory Clinic, Beaumont Hospital). Comorbidities: ischemic heart disease with two prior myocardial infarctions, gastro‑oesophageal reflux disease, orthostatic hypotension (direct complication of Parkinson’s disease and several medications). Room 11. Morning assessment at 09:15 h on Monday.

Medication regimen:  
- Sinemet 125 mg PO qid at 06:00, 10:00, 14:00, 18:00 (timing‑critical).  
- Madopar dispersible 62.5 mg PO PRN for breakthrough off episodes.  
- Ropinirole prolonged‑release 4 mg PO nightly at bedtime.  
- Domperidone 10 mg PO TID, 30 min before meals (gastroparesis).  
- Omeprazole 20 mg PO daily (GERD).  
- Aspirin 75 mg PO daily (antiplatelet).  
- Bisoprolol 2.5 mg PO daily (heart failure).  
- Furosemide 20 mg PO daily (mild fluid retention).

The 06:00 Sinemet dose was administered at exactly 06:00 h this morning by night nurse Eimear (documented). The 09:00 medication round was given at exactly 09:00 h by the author. The 14:00 and 18:00 Sinemet doses will be administered at their precise scheduled times; delays of 30–45 min may precipitate a severe motor off state characterised by freezing, rigidity, inability to walk and extreme distress.

Night assessment documented multiple episodes of vivid and frightening nightmares (recorded by night nurse Eimear).

conker woke screaming on at least three occasions and was found sitting upright in bed in obvious distress. on one occasion he appeared to be striking out at an unseen person in the room and eimear had to take care not to be struck when she approached. when he became more alert he described very detailed dreams about being chased and about his family being harmed. these dreams were extremely vivid and convincing to him and he took some time on each occasion to orient himself to reality after waking. this pattern is consistent with rapid eye movement sleep behaviour disorder which is a well recognised complication of parkinson's disease and parkinson's disease dementia. eimear sat with conker after each episode for approximately twenty minutes until he resettled. the dreams are not new but eimear notes they appear to have increased in frequency and intensity over the past week. i have reviewed the clinical notes and have confirmed that the consultant neurologist dr byrne discussed this issue at conker's last review three months ago and documented a plan to consider melatonin two milligrams at bedtime as a first line treatment for the rem sleep behaviour disorder but that the prescription was not written at that visit. i have contacted dr byrne's secretary to request that this prescription be issued and i have also written a query in the medical notes requesting a response from the ward medical team. this morning conker is in a significant motor off state. his tremor in the right hand is severe and rhythmic at approximately five to six hertz making any fine motor task such as brushing teeth, holding cutlery, or buttoning clothing impossible without full assistance. he has experienced three freezing episodes at doorway transitions this morning between seven and nine fifteen. on each occasion i used the laser cue wristband device which projects a red line on the floor and which conker finds more reliably effective than the sticky tape threshold method. two of the three episodes responded within thirty to sixty seconds to the laser cue.

the third episode lasted approximately four minutes despite the cue and i also used auditory cueing by saying left, left, left in a rhythmic pattern which eventually triggered the step. conker was visibly distressed and frustrated by the duration of this third freezing episode and banged his hand on the door frame in frustration. i acknowledged his frustration verbally and reassured him that we would work on this together. his voice today is very hypophonic and mildly dysarthric. conversational speech is difficult to hear even at close range and i need to ask him to repeat himself several times. i have noted that this is slightly worse than last week and will document it in the communications section for the speech and language therapist to review at the next session. his blood pressure lying at nine am is one hundred and eight over sixty four. sitting blood pressure taken after two minutes in an upright seated position is eighty nine over fifty eight. this represents a drop of nineteen millimetres of mercury systolic on position change which meets the definition of orthostatic hypotension and is a significant fall risk. i have advised conker never to get up from bed or chair without pressing the call bell and have ensured all staff are verbally briefed this morning that no transfers should occur without a nurse present and that all position changes must be performed very slowly with a two to three minute sit-on-edge pause before standing. i have documented this in the nursing care plan. his oral intake this morning was poor. he managed half a bowl of porridge and one cup of tea. his drooling and hypersalivation is very prominent today and he finds it very humiliating. he had to wipe his face multiple times during breakfast and became tearful and said he did not want anyone to see him like this. i sat with him and provided extra napkins and worked at a very slow unhurried pace to try to reduce his embarrassment.

the speech and language therapist had previously recommended glycopyrrolate as a medical treatment for the hypersalivation but dr byrne had not issued the prescription. i have again added this to the outstanding medical review query list. conker's weight today is sixty three point zero kilograms which is down from sixty three point eight kilograms one week ago. this is a loss of eight hundred grams in seven days which is clinically significant and consistent with his poor oral intake pattern. i have updated the malnutrition universal screening tool score and the dietitian has been notified.

tierney thomas room 8B this is tierney thomas room eight b it is one pm thursday palliative care assessment. tierney is a ninety one year old woman with end stage congestive cardiac failure new york heart association class four, chronic kidney disease stage four, type two diabetes, and severe osteoporosis with documented vertebral compression fractures at thoracic six and lumbar one. she is being managed under a fully established palliative care framework with a comprehensive advance care directive signed and witnessed in september of last year. the directive specifies no hospital transfer, no cardiopulmonary resuscitation, no invasive procedures including no nasogastric or intravenous feeding, no blood taking beyond what is needed for immediate comfort management, and no escalation of treatment for acute deterioration. the family specifically tierneys daughter siobhan thomas and her son declan thomas are fully informed and completely supportive of this framework. both have expressed on multiple occasions that they want tierney to die in comfort and dignity in this facility surrounded by her family and the staff who know her. i met with siobhan this morning at ten thirty for a formal family meeting lasting approximately fifty minutes. siobhan raised several concerns and observations that i want to document carefully. she has noticed over the past week that her mothers breathing has changed significantly. she describes it as more laboured with longer pauses and a different pattern from before. she also described hearing a rattling or gurgling sound in tierneys chest when she visits which she says began approximately three or four days ago. she asked me directly whether her mother is dying. i answered this question honestly and compassionately. i told her that yes i believe tierney is entering the final phase of her life, that the changes she is observing including the breathing pattern and the secretions are consistent with the dying process, and that the medical and nursing team are focused entirely on keeping tierney comfortable and free from pain and distress. siobhan cried during this part of our conversation and i provided tissues and sat with her quietly for several minutes.

she then said she was grateful for the honesty and said she had been feeling she needed permission to acknowledge what she was seeing. declan has been less available due to work commitments but siobhan has been sharing information with him by phone. i have offered both siblings access to the pastoral care coordinator and the bereavement support service and siobhan has said she would like the chaplain to visit today if possible. i have contacted the chaplain who will visit at four pm. on my one pm clinical assessment tierney is lying at thirty degrees elevation in bed. her eyes are partially open but she does not track movement or respond to her name consistently. she occasionally opens her eyes briefly when her hand is held and squeezed gently which i interpret as a retained awareness at some level. her breathing today shows a very marked cheyne stokes pattern with crescendo phases lasting approximately twenty seconds followed by apnoeic pauses of eight to fifteen seconds. this is more pronounced than at yesterday's assessment when the apnoeic pauses were shorter at five to eight seconds. i have documented this progression. dr egan the palliative care consultant was consulted by phone this morning and reviewed his notes from his in-person visit on wednesday. he has increased the subcutaneous morphine in the continuous syringe driver from twenty to twenty five milligrams over twenty four hours to address the visible respiratory distress and subtle facial grimacing seen during the crescendo breathing phases. he has also increased the midazolam in the syringe driver from two point five milligrams to five milligrams over twenty four hours to address the restlessness and agitation that has been escalating over the past forty eight hours. the syringe driver was reloaded with the new doses at twelve noon today by the palliative care clinical nurse specialist catherine who made an unplanned visit to the unit specifically to assist with this management adjustment. i have confirmed that the syringe driver is running correctly at one pm with the correct medications and doses loaded and the site is clean.

the secretions which siobhan described as rattling are audible in the room at about thirty centimetres distance from tierneys face. they are wet secretions caused by the inability to clear saliva and bronchial secretions due to the progressive loss of muscle tone and swallow reflex. hyoscine butylbromide twenty milligrams subcutaneously was administered at nine am and again at twelve noon today as prescribed for secretion management. at one pm the secretions are present but less audible than at nine am suggesting some response to the hyoscine. i have carefully explained to siobhan and separately to declan by phone that the secretion noise does not indicate that tierney is in distress or struggling to breathe and that the sounds are much more distressing for observers than they are for the patient herself. this is well evidenced in the palliative care literature and both family members received this explanation with some degree of relief. tierneys oral care is being provided every two hours using pink oral sponge sticks moistened with water. her lips are cracked and dry and i have been applying a thick barrier lip balm after each oral care session. her oral mucosa shows some white patches which i have swabbed and sent for culture to rule out oral candidiasis which is common in the dying phase. nystatin oral suspension is prescribed and will be commenced if candida is confirmed. tierney has not taken any food or fluid orally for four days now. she has had no urine output for the past eighteen hours and the long term urethral catheter which has been in place for three months shows no drainage. i have flushed the catheter gently with ten millilitres of normal saline to ensure it is not blocked and confirmed the catheter remains in the bladder but the kidneys appear to have essentially ceased functioning. this is an expected and recognised finding in the final hours to days of life and is consistent with the profound multi-organ failure she is experiencing.

i have documented this and informed the family that this represents a further sign that tierney is very close to the end of her life. the family are staying close and siobhan has asked whether she should bring declan tonight in case tierney does not survive until morning and i have gently said that yes if declan is able to be there i would encourage it. this conversation has been documented.

Sadhbh Menon, 79‑year‑old female, resident in Room 4, assessed at 1500 hrs Wednesday. Comorbidities: type 1 diabetes mellitus on basal‑bolus insulin, hypertension, chronic atrial fibrillation on apixaban, CKD stage 3b, moderate frailty syndrome, and five UTIs in the past 12 months; currently on prophylactic trimethoprim 100 mg PO nightly.  

Patient self‑removed IV cannula from left forearm at approximately 1300 hrs while receiving IV normal saline 100 mL/hr (prescribed by Dr Ahmed for dehydration secondary to reduced oral intake over the past 5 days, with dry mucous membranes, decreased skin turgor, concentrated urine, and creatinine rise from 125 to 152 µmol/L on Monday’s labs). Patient was briefly out of the room; at 1305 hrs staff found the IV line disconnected, fluid dripping onto the mattress, cannula lying on the bedside, and patient seated calmly eating a digestive biscuit, stating the drip fell out by itself. Inspection of the insertion site revealed a 2‑mm tear at the inferior edge, consistent with forcible self‑removal.  

This is the second self‑removal of IV cannula during this admission (first occurred last Thursday at ~1100 hrs).

Patient self-removed IV cannula; refused reinsertion for approximately six hours, then consented at 17:00 after a lengthy conversation with Dr Ahmed. Event documented as patient self‑removal of IV access; clinical incident report completed; Dr Ahmed consulted by phone. Dr Ahmed reviewed fluctuating cooperation with IV therapy and morning labs (creatinine 145 µmol/L, modest improvement from 152 µmol/L on Monday, slightly above baseline) and decided to step down rehydration to aggressive oral hydration, target 2000 mL/day, with repeat biochemistry tomorrow. If renal function deteriorates or oral intake remains inadequate, IV access will be reconsidered and a formal best‑interest discussion will be held. Plan explained to patient; patient encouraged to increase fluid intake and agreed to try.

Fasting blood glucose at 07:00 was 3.1 mmol/L (hypoglycaemia <3.9 mmol/L) with tremor, sweating, and mild confusion. Administered 15 g rapid‑acting carbohydrate via 150 mL fresh orange juice at 07:10. Blood glucose rechecked at 07:25: 5.2 mmol/L, symptoms resolved. Morning insulin administered thereafter.

Novorapid (insulin aspart) 8 U administered at 07:30 with breakfast. Pre‑lunch blood glucose at 12:00 was 18.6 mmol/L – markedly hyperglycaemic, representing a dramatic swing from hypoglycaemia at 07:00 to hyperglycaemia at noon in the context of variable intake and acute illness, indicating an unstable glycaemic pattern. Diabetes specialist nurse Siobhan reviewed the resident by phone today; Dr Ahmed verbally authorised temporary dose reductions: breakfast Novorapid reduced to 4 U, lunch Novorapid reduced to 4 U, dinner Novorapid reduced to 6 U, Lantus (insulin glargine) to remain at 22 U at bedtime. Adjustments documented in the MAR with clinical rationale. Adjusted breakfast Novorapid 4 U administered at 07:30 with breakfast. Adjusted lunch Novorapid 4 U administered at 12:30 with lunch. Adjusted dinner Novorapid 6 U to be given at approximately 18:00. 15:00 BM 11.4 mmol/L – improved but above target; hourly monitoring will continue until values consistently 6–10 mmol/L.

Skin tear identified on right forearm at 14:30 during afternoon personal care. Approx. 4.5 cm length × 1.5 cm width, Category 2 with partial flap. Wound edges clean; minimal bleeding controlled with direct pressure. Wound irrigated with normal saline, partial flap realigned, silicone foam bordered dressing applied by staff. Right forearm elevated on pillow to reduce oedema.

Resident Sadhbv cannot recall injuring herself, which is not unusual for skin tears in frail elderly patients due to fragile skin and minor unnoticed contact. She is on apixaban, which will prolong any bleeding tendency; this is documented in the wound chart as a relevant factor. Completed full skin integrity assessment today: arms, shins, and dorsal hands all show characteristic changes of skin fragility. Care plan updated to include protective measures: long‑sleeve loose cotton garments at all times; padded arm guards while in wheelchair; all jewellery removed from wrists and hands permanently during her stay; bed rails and wheelchair armrests padded with foam protectors; staff to always use slide sheets for repositioning and never pull on limbs. Measures communicated verbally at the nursing afternoon stand‑up and documented in the care plan.  

Renal function in the context of today’s events is being managed with oral hydration strategy as above. Urine output from 07:00 to 15:00 today is 310 ml (low); urine is dark yellow and concentrated. Fluid balance chart is being closely monitored; will contact Dr Ahmed if output does not improve by the 18:00 check or if clinical status deteriorates.  

Atrial fibrillation is rate‑controlled at approximately 80–90 bpm. Last echocardiogram (6 months ago) showed moderate left ventricular hypertrophy with preserved ejection fraction. Apixaban 2.5 mg BID administered this morning; next dose due at 18:00.  

BP at 15:00 is 148/84 mmHg – within acceptable range for her managed hypertension target of <150/90 mmHg.
